title: Deforestation

infobox.value: or **forest clearance** is the removal and destruction of a [forest](/wiki/Forest "Forest") or stand of trees from land that is then [converted](/wiki/Land_conversion "Land conversion") to non-forest use.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Deforestation can involve conversion of forest land to [farms](/wiki/Farm "Farm"), [ranches](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch"), or [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") use. About 31% of Earth's land surface is covered by [forests](/wiki/Forest "Forest") at present.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This is one-third less than the [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") before the expansion of agriculture, with half of that loss occurring in the last century.[[3]](#cite_note-3) On average 2,400 trees are cut down each minute.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Estimates vary widely as to the extent of deforestation in the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) In 2019, nearly a third of the overall tree cover loss, or 3.8 million hectares, occurred within humid tropical [primary forests](/wiki/Primary_forests "Primary forests"). These are areas of mature [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") that are especially important for [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") and [carbon storage](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration").[[7]](#cite_note-Guy-2020-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Conversion of forest to non-forest for human use

"Forest clearing" redirects here. For a gap in a forest, see [Glade (geography)](/wiki/Glade_(geography) "Glade (geography)").

"Deforest" redirects here. For other uses, see [DeForest (disambiguation)](/wiki/DeForest_(disambiguation) "DeForest (disambiguation)").

For "deforestation" in computer science, see [Deforestation (computer science)](/wiki/Deforestation_(computer_science) "Deforestation (computer science)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Hymenaea%2C_Julho-2016_%2829399454651%29.jpg/250px-Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Hymenaea%2C_Julho-2016_%2829399454651%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Hymenaea,_Julho-2016_(29399454651).jpg)

[Deforestation of the Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Deforestation_of_the_Amazon_rainforest "Deforestation of the Amazon rainforest") in Brazil's [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão") state, 2016

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Riau_palm_oil_2007.jpg/250px-Riau_palm_oil_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riau_palm_oil_2007.jpg)

Deforestation in Riau province, Sumatra, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") to make way for an [oil palm](/wiki/Oil_palm "Oil palm") plantation in 2007.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Hillside_deforestation_in_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg/250px-Hillside_deforestation_in_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hillside_deforestation_in_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)

Deforestation in the city of Rio de Janeiro in Brazil's [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") state, 2009

**Deforestation** or **forest clearance** is the removal and destruction of a [forest](/wiki/Forest "Forest") or stand of trees from land that is then [converted](/wiki/Land_conversion "Land conversion") to non-forest use.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Deforestation can involve conversion of forest land to [farms](/wiki/Farm "Farm"), [ranches](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch"), or [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") use. About 31% of Earth's land surface is covered by [forests](/wiki/Forest "Forest") at present.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This is one-third less than the [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") before the expansion of agriculture, with half of that loss occurring in the last century.[[3]](#cite_note-3) On average 2,400 trees are cut down each minute.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Estimates vary widely as to the extent of deforestation in the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) In 2019, nearly a third of the overall tree cover loss, or 3.8 million hectares, occurred within humid tropical [primary forests](/wiki/Primary_forests "Primary forests"). These are areas of mature [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") that are especially important for [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") and [carbon storage](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration").[[7]](#cite_note-Guy-2020-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)

By far, the direct cause of most deforestation is agriculture.[[9]](#cite_note-UNFCC-2007-9) More than 80% of deforestation was attributed to agriculture in 2012.[[10]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily-2018-10) Forests are being converted to plantations for [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil"), [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") and various other popular products.[[11]](#cite_note-WWF-2020-11) Livestock [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") also drives deforestation. Further drivers are the [wood industry](/wiki/Wood_industry "Wood industry") ([logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging")), [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") and [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining"). The [effects of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change") are another cause via the increased risk of [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire") (see [deforestation and climate change](/wiki/Deforestation_and_climate_change "Deforestation and climate change")).

Deforestation results in [habitat destruction](/wiki/Habitat_destruction "Habitat destruction") which in turn leads to [biodiversity loss](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss"). Deforestation also leads to [extinction](/wiki/Extinction "Extinction") of animals and plants, changes to the local climate, and displacement of [indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") who live in forests. Deforested regions often also suffer from other environmental problems such as [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification") and [soil erosion](/wiki/Soil_erosion "Soil erosion").

Another problem is that deforestation reduces the uptake of carbon dioxide ([carbon sequestration](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration")) from the atmosphere. This reduces the potential of forests to assist with [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation"). The role of forests in capturing and storing carbon and mitigating climate change is also important for the agricultural sector.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12) The reason for this linkage is that the [effects of climate change on agriculture](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_agriculture "Effects of climate change on agriculture") pose new risks to global [food systems](/wiki/Food_system "Food system").[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)

Since 1990, it is estimated that some [420 million hectares](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(area)#108_to_1014_square_metres "Orders of magnitude (area)") of forest have been lost through [conversion](/wiki/Land_conversion "Land conversion") to other [land uses](/wiki/Land_use "Land use"), although the rate of deforestation has decreased over the past three decades. Between 2015 and 2020, the rate of deforestation was estimated at 10 million hectares per year, down from 16 million hectares per year in the 1990s. The area of primary forest worldwide has decreased by over 80 million hectares since 1990. More than 100 million hectares of forests are adversely affected by forest fires, pests, diseases, [invasive species](/wiki/Invasive_species "Invasive species"), [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") and adverse weather events.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Definition

[![This screen shot shows a map that highlights countries based on their net change rate of forest area. Areas that appear more blue have a higher net change rate than areas that appear tan. Brown areas indicate a net loss of forest area.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Annual_change_in_forest_area%2C_world_map%2C_OWID.png/250px-Annual_change_in_forest_area%2C_world_map%2C_OWID.png)](/wiki/File:Annual_change_in_forest_area,_world_map,_OWID.png)

Forest area net change rate per country in 2020

Deforestation is defined as the conversion of forest to other land uses (regardless of whether it is human-induced).[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

*Deforestation* and *forest area net change* are not the same: the latter is the sum of all forest losses (deforestation) and all forest gains (forest expansion) in a given period. Net change, therefore, can be positive or negative, depending on whether gains exceed losses, or vice versa.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

## Current status by continent, region, country

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Annual-deforestation.svg/250px-Annual-deforestation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Annual-deforestation.svg)

Annual deforestation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Annual-change-forest-area.svg/250px-Annual-change-forest-area.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Annual-change-forest-area.svg)

Annual change in forest area

The FAO estimates that the global forest carbon stock has decreased 0.9%, and tree cover 4.2% between 1990 and 2020.[[15]](#cite_note-FAO-2020-15): 16, 52

Changes in forest carbon stock by region  
Figures in gigatons[[15]](#cite_note-FAO-2020-15): 52, table 43

| Region | 1990 | 2020 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Europe (including Russia) | 158.7 | 172.4 |
| North America | 136.6 | 140.0 |
| Africa | 94.3 | 80.9 |
| South and Southeast Asia combined | 45.8 | 41.5 |
| Oceania | 33.4 | 33.1 |
| Central America | 5.0 | 4.1 |
| South America | 161.8 | 144.8 |

As of 2019 there is still disagreement about whether the global forest is shrinking or not: "While above-ground biomass carbon stocks are estimated to be declining in the tropics, they are increasing globally due to increasing stocks in temperate and boreal forest.[[16]](#cite_note-16): 385

[Deforestation in many countries](/wiki/Deforestation_by_region "Deforestation by region")—both naturally occurring[[17]](#cite_note-17) and [human-induced](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment")—is an ongoing issue.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Between 2000 and 2012, 2.3 million square kilometres (890,000 square miles) of forests around the world were cut down.[[19]](#cite_note-nature1-19) Deforestation and forest degradation continue to take place at alarming rates, which contributes significantly to the ongoing [loss of biodiversity](/wiki/Loss_of_biodiversity "Loss of biodiversity").[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Land-use-of-different-diets-Poore-Nemecek.png/250px-Land-use-of-different-diets-Poore-Nemecek.png)](/wiki/File:Land-use-of-different-diets-Poore-Nemecek.png)

The amount of globally needed agricultural land would be reduced by three quarters if the entire population adopted a [vegan](/wiki/Veganism "Veganism") diet.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Deforestation is more extreme in tropical and subtropical forests in emerging economies. More than half of all plant and land animal species in the world live in [tropical forests](/wiki/Tropical_forest "Tropical forest").[[21]](#cite_note-Rainforest_Facts-21) As a result of deforestation, only 6.2 million square kilometres (2.4 million square miles) remain of the original 16 million square kilometres (6 million square miles) of tropical rainforest that formerly covered the Earth.[[19]](#cite_note-nature1-19) More than 3.6 million hectares of virgin tropical forest was lost in 2018.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The global annual net loss of trees is estimated to be approximately 10 billion.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) According to the [Global Forest Resources Assessment 2020](/wiki/Global_Forest_Resources_Assessment_(FRA) "Global Forest Resources Assessment (FRA)") the global average annual deforested land in the 2015–2020 demi-decade was 10 million hectares and the average annual forest area net loss in the 2000–2010 decade was 4.7 million hectares.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14) The world has lost 178 million ha of forest since 1990, which is an area about the size of Libya.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

An analysis of global deforestation patterns in 2021 showed that patterns of trade, production, and consumption drive deforestation rates in complex ways. While the location of deforestation can be mapped, it does not always match where the commodity is consumed. For example, consumption patterns in [G7 countries](/wiki/G7_countries "G7 countries") are estimated to cause an average loss of 3.9 trees per person per year. In other words, deforestation can be directly related to imports—for example, coffee.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 2023, the [Global Forest Watch](/wiki/Global_Forest_Watch "Global Forest Watch") reported a 9% decline in tropical primary forest loss compared to the previous year, with significant regional reductions in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") overshadowed by increases elsewhere, leading to a 3.2% rise in global deforestation. Massive wildfires in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), exacerbated by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), contributed to a 24% increase in global tree cover loss, highlighting the ongoing threats to forests essential for [carbon storage](/wiki/Carbon_storage "Carbon storage") and [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"). Despite some progress, the overall trends in forest destruction and climate impacts remain off track.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The [IPCC Sixth Assessment Report](/wiki/IPCC_Sixth_Assessment_Report "IPCC Sixth Assessment Report") stated in 2022: "Over 420 million ha of forest were lost to deforestation from 1990 to 2020; more than 90% of that loss took place in tropical areas (high confidence), threatening biodiversity, environmental services, livelihoods of forest communities and resilience to climate shocks (high confidence)."[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Rates of deforestation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/1700-_Tropical_rainforest_and_woodland_loss_-_Int%27l_Geosphere-Biosphere_Programme_%28IGBP%29.svg/250px-1700-_Tropical_rainforest_and_woodland_loss_-_Int%27l_Geosphere-Biosphere_Programme_%28IGBP%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1700-_Tropical_rainforest_and_woodland_loss_-_Int%27l_Geosphere-Biosphere_Programme_(IGBP).svg)

The period since 1950 has brought "the most rapid transformation of the human relationship with the natural world in the history of humankind".[[29]](#cite_note-IGBP_Steffen_2004-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/8000_BCE%2B_Loss_of_forest_and_grassland_to_grazing_and_crops.svg/250px-8000_BCE%2B_Loss_of_forest_and_grassland_to_grazing_and_crops.svg.png)](/wiki/File:8000_BCE%2B_Loss_of_forest_and_grassland_to_grazing_and_crops.svg)

Through 2018, humans have reduced forest area by ~30% and grasslands/shrubs by ~68%, to make way for livestock grazing and crops for humans.[[30]](#cite_note-LandUseChange_8000BCE-30)

Global deforestation[[31]](#cite_note-31) sharply accelerated around 1852.[[32]](#cite_note-Wilson-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) As of 1947, the planet had 15 to 16 million km2 (5.8 to 6.2 million mi2) of mature [tropical forests](/wiki/Tropical_forest "Tropical forest"),[[34]](#cite_note-worldbook-34) but by 2015, it was estimated that about half of these had been destroyed.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[21]](#cite_note-Rainforest_Facts-21)[[36]](#cite_note-Nielsen-36) Total land coverage by tropical rainforests decreased from 14% to 6%. Much of this loss happened between 1960 and 1990, when 20% of all tropical rainforests were destroyed. At this rate, extinction of such forests is projected to occur by the mid-21st century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the early 2000s, some scientists predicted that unless significant measures (such as seeking out and protecting old growth forests that have not been disturbed)[[34]](#cite_note-worldbook-34) are taken on a worldwide basis, by 2030 there will only be 10% remaining,[[32]](#cite_note-Wilson-32)[[36]](#cite_note-Nielsen-36) with another 10% [in a degraded condition](/wiki/Degraded_forest "Degraded forest").[[32]](#cite_note-Wilson-32) 80% will have been lost, and with them hundreds of thousands of irreplaceable species.[[32]](#cite_note-Wilson-32)

Estimates vary widely as to the extent of deforestation in the tropics.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) In 2019, the world lost nearly 12 million hectares of tree cover. Nearly a third of that loss, 3.8 million hectares, occurred within humid tropical primary forests, areas of mature rainforest that are especially important for biodiversity and carbon storage. This is equivalent to losing an area of primary forest the size of a football pitch every six seconds.[[7]](#cite_note-Guy-2020-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)

#### Rates of change

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/1990-_Average_annual_forest_area_net_change%2C_globally%2C_and_by_region_and_decade.svg/250px-1990-_Average_annual_forest_area_net_change%2C_globally%2C_and_by_region_and_decade.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1990-_Average_annual_forest_area_net_change,_globally,_and_by_region_and_decade.svg)

In decades since 1990, South America and Africa have shown the greatest loss of forest area, with global net loss in the 2010s still about 60% of the 1990s value.[[37]](#cite_note-FAO_2020-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/20210331_Global_tree_cover_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg/250px-20210331_Global_tree_cover_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20210331_Global_tree_cover_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg)

The rate of global tree cover loss has approximately doubled since 2001, to an annual loss approaching an area the size of Italy.[[38]](#cite_note-Mongabay_WRI-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2002-_Tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_annual_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg/250px-2002-_Tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_annual_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2002-_Tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_annual_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg)

Loss of [primary (old-growth)](/wiki/Old-growth_forest "Old-growth forest") forest in the tropics has continued its upward trend, with fire-related losses contributing an increasing portion.[[39]](#cite_note-WRI_thru2024-39)

A 2002 analysis of satellite imagery suggested that the rate of deforestation in the humid tropics (approximately 5.8 million hectares per year) was roughly 23% lower than the most commonly quoted rates.[[40]](#cite_note-40) A 2005 report by the United Nations [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO) estimated that although the Earth's total forest area continued to decrease at about 13 million hectares per year, the global rate of deforestation had been slowing.[[41]](#cite_note-pantropical-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) On the other hand, a 2005 analysis of satellite images reveals that [deforestation of the Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Deforestation_of_the_Amazon_rainforest "Deforestation of the Amazon rainforest") is twice as fast as scientists previously estimated.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

From 2010 to 2015, worldwide forest area decreased by 3.3 million ha per year, according to [FAO](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization"). During this five-year period, the biggest forest area loss occurred in the tropics, particularly in South America and Africa. Per capita forest area decline was also greatest in the tropics and subtropics but is occurring in every climatic domain (except in the temperate) as populations increase.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

An estimated 420 million ha of forest has been lost worldwide through deforestation since 1990, but the rate of forest loss has declined substantially. In the most recent five-year period (2015–2020), the annual rate of deforestation was estimated at 10 million ha, down from 12 million ha in 2010–2015.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/2021_Top_ten_countries_for_tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg/250px-2021_Top_ten_countries_for_tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2021_Top_ten_countries_for_tropical_primary_forest_loss_-_World_Resources_Institute.svg)

Home to much of the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest"), Brazil's tropical [primary (old-growth)](/wiki/Old-growth_forest "Old-growth forest") forest loss greatly exceeds that of other countries.[[46]](#cite_note-WRI_20240404-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/20220910_Amazon_deforestation_and_degradation%2C_by_country_-_Amazon_Watch.svg/250px-20220910_Amazon_deforestation_and_degradation%2C_by_country_-_Amazon_Watch.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20220910_Amazon_deforestation_and_degradation,_by_country_-_Amazon_Watch.svg)

Overall, 20% of the Amazon rainforest has been "transformed" (deforested) and another 6% has been "highly degraded", causing Amazon Watch to warn that the Amazonia is in the midst of a tipping point crisis.[[47]](#cite_note-AmazonWatch_202209-47)

Africa had the largest annual rate of net forest loss in 2010–2020, at 3.9 million ha, followed by South America, at 2.6 million ha. The rate of net forest loss has increased in Africa in each of the three decades since 1990. It has declined substantially in South America, however, to about half the rate in 2010–2020 compared with 2000–2010. Asia had the highest net gain of forest area in 2010–2020, followed by Oceania and Europe. Nevertheless, both Europe and Asia recorded substantially lower rates of net gain in 2010–2020 than in 2000–2010. Oceania experienced net losses of forest area in the decades 1990–2000 and 2000–2010.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

Some claim that rainforests are being destroyed at an ever-quickening pace.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The London-based Rainforest Foundation notes that "the UN figure is based on a definition of forest as being an area with as little as 10% actual tree cover, which would therefore include areas that are actually savanna-like ecosystems and badly damaged forests".[[49]](#cite_note-Butler-49) Other critics of the FAO data point out that they do not distinguish between forest types,[[50]](#cite_note-50) and that they are based largely on reporting from [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") departments of individual countries,[[51]](#cite_note-51) which do not take into account unofficial activities like illegal logging.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Despite these uncertainties, there is agreement that destruction of rainforests remains a significant environmental problem.

The rate of net forest loss declined from 7.8 million ha per year in the decade 1990–2000 to 5.2 million ha per year in 2000–2010 and 4.7 million ha per year in 2010–2020. The rate of decline of net forest loss slowed in the most recent decade due to a reduction in the rate of forest expansion.[[14]](#cite_note-GFRA2020-14)

#### Reforestation and afforestation

Main articles: [Reforestation](/wiki/Reforestation "Reforestation") and [Afforestation](/wiki/Afforestation "Afforestation")

In many parts of the world, especially in East Asian countries, [reforestation](/wiki/Reforestation "Reforestation") and [afforestation](/wiki/Afforestation "Afforestation") are increasing the area of forested lands.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The amount of forest has increased in 22 of the world's 50 most forested nations. Asia as a whole gained 1 million [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") of forest between 2000 and 2005. Tropical forest in El Salvador expanded more than 20% between 1992 and 2001. Based on these trends, one study projects that global forestation will increase by 10%—an area the size of India—by 2050.[[54]](#cite_note-Owen-54) 36% of globally planted forest area is in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") – around 950,000 square kilometers. From those 87% are in China.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Status by region

Main article: [Deforestation by continent](/wiki/Deforestation_by_continent "Deforestation by continent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Annual_forest_area_net_change%2C_by_country%2C_1990%E2%80%932025.svg/250px-Annual_forest_area_net_change%2C_by_country%2C_1990%E2%80%932025.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Annual_forest_area_net_change,_by_country,_1990%E2%80%932025.svg)

Annual forest area net change, by country, 1990–2025

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Global_annual_net_change_of_naturally_regenerating_forest%2C_1990%E2%80%932025.svg/250px-Global_annual_net_change_of_naturally_regenerating_forest%2C_1990%E2%80%932025.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Global_annual_net_change_of_naturally_regenerating_forest,_1990%E2%80%932025.svg)

Global annual net change of naturally regenerating forest, 1990–2025

Rates of deforestation vary around the world. Up to 90% of [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa")'s coastal rainforests have disappeared since 1900.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") has lost 90% of its eastern rainforests.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) In [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), about 88% of the rainforests have been lost.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Indonesia "Deforestation in Indonesia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Burma](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Malaysia "Deforestation in Malaysia"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), China, [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Sri_Lanka "Deforestation in Sri Lanka"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Liberia](/wiki/Liberia "Liberia"), [Guinea](/wiki/Guinea "Guinea"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") and the [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast"), have lost large areas of their rainforest.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Amazon_fire_satellite_image.png/250px-Amazon_fire_satellite_image.png)](/wiki/File:Amazon_fire_satellite_image.png)

[Satellite imagery](/wiki/Earth_observation_satellite#Environmental_monitoring "Earth observation satellite") of locations of the [2019 Amazon rainforest wildfires](/wiki/2019_Amazon_rainforest_wildfires "2019 Amazon rainforest wildfires") as detected by [MODIS](/wiki/MODIS "MODIS") from August 15 to August 22, 2019

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Pichincha1a.jpg/250px-Pichincha1a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pichincha1a.jpg)

Deforestation in [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador").

Much of what remains of the world's rainforests is in the [Amazon basin](/wiki/Amazon_basin "Amazon basin"), where the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") covers approximately 4 million square kilometres.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Some 80% of the deforestation of the Amazon can be attributed to cattle ranching,[[63]](#cite_note-63) as Brazil is the largest exporter of beef in the world.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Amazon region has become one of the largest cattle ranching territories in the world.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The regions with the highest tropical deforestation rate between 2000 and 2005 were [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America")—which lost 1.3% of its forests each year—and tropical Asia.[[49]](#cite_note-Butler-49) In [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), two-thirds of lowland tropical forests have been turned into pasture since 1950 and 40% of all the rainforests have been lost in the last 40 years.[[66]](#cite_note-ru.org-66) [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") has lost 90–95% of its [Mata Atlântica](/wiki/Mata_Atl%C3%A2ntica "Mata Atlântica") forest.[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Deforestation in Brazil](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Brazil "Deforestation in Brazil") increased by 88% for the month of June 2019, as compared with the previous year.[[68]](#cite_note-68) However, Brazil still destroyed 1.3 million hectares in 2019.[[7]](#cite_note-Guy-2020-7) [Brazil](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Brazil "Deforestation in Brazil") is one of several countries that have declared their deforestation a national emergency.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)
[Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") was losing its natural semi-humid forests in the country's western regions at a rate of 15,000 hectares at a randomly studied 2-month period in 2010.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In 2009, Paraguay's parliament refused to pass a law that would have stopped cutting of natural forests altogether.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

As of 2007, [less than 50% of Haiti's forests remained](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Haiti "Deforestation in Haiti").[[73]](#cite_note-73)

From 2015 to 2019, the rate of [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Deforestation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") in the Democratic Republic of the Congo doubled.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 2021, deforestation of the [Congolese rainforest](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests") increased by 5%.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The [World Wildlife Fund](/wiki/World_Wildlife_Fund "World Wildlife Fund")'s [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") project catalogues habitat types throughout the world, including habitat loss such as deforestation, showing for example that even in the rich forests of parts of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") such as the [Mid-Continental Canadian forests](/wiki/Mid-Continental_Canadian_forests "Mid-Continental Canadian forests") of the prairie provinces half of the forest cover has been lost or altered.

In 2011, [Conservation International](/wiki/Conservation_International "Conservation International") listed the top 10 most endangered forests, characterized by having all lost 90% or more of their original [habitat](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat"), and each harboring at least 1500 [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") plant species (species found nowhere else in the world).[[76]](#cite_note-ci2011-76)

As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Deforestation&action=edit), it is estimated that 70% of the world's forests are within one kilometer of a forest edge, where they are most prone to human interference and destruction.[[77]](#cite_note-IPCC_AR6-77)[[78]](#cite_note-Haddad2015-78)
