## Causes

See also: [Deforestation of the Amazon rainforest § Causes of deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation_of_the_Amazon_rainforest#Causes_of_deforestation "Deforestation of the Amazon rainforest"), and [Deforestation in Brazil § Causes](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Brazil#Causes "Deforestation in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Drivers_of_deforestation_and_forest_degradation_by_region%2C_2000%E2%80%932010.svg/250px-Drivers_of_deforestation_and_forest_degradation_by_region%2C_2000%E2%80%932010.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Drivers_of_deforestation_and_forest_degradation_by_region,_2000%E2%80%932010.svg)

Drivers of deforestation and forest degradation by region, 2000–2010[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Drivers_of_tropical_deforestration.png/250px-Drivers_of_tropical_deforestration.png)](/wiki/File:Drivers_of_tropical_deforestration.png)

Drivers of tropical deforestration

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Riau_deforestation_2006.jpg/250px-Riau_deforestation_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riau_deforestation_2006.jpg)

The last batch of sawnwood from the [peat forest](/wiki/Peat_swamp_forest "Peat swamp forest") in [Indragiri Hulu](/wiki/Indragiri_Hulu "Indragiri Hulu"), [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Indonesia "Deforestation in Indonesia"). Deforestation for [oil palm](/wiki/Elaeis_guineensis "Elaeis guineensis") plantation.

[Agricultural expansion](/wiki/Agricultural_expansion "Agricultural expansion") continues to be the main driver of deforestation and forest fragmentation and the associated loss of forest biodiversity.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12) Large-scale commercial agriculture (primarily cattle ranching and cultivation of soya bean and oil palm) accounted for 40 percent of tropical deforestation between 2000 and 2010, and local subsistence agriculture for another 33 percent.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12) Trees are cut down for use as building material, timber or sold as fuel (sometimes in the form of [charcoal](/wiki/Charcoal "Charcoal") or [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber")), while cleared land is used as [pasture](/wiki/Pasture "Pasture") for [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") and agricultural crops.

The vast majority of agricultural activity resulting in deforestation is [subsidized by government tax revenue](/wiki/Agricultural_subsidy "Agricultural subsidy").[[89]](#cite_note-89) Disregard of ascribed value, lax [forest management](/wiki/Forest_management "Forest management"), and deficient environmental laws are some of the factors that lead to large-scale deforestation.

The types of drivers vary greatly depending on the region in which they take place. The regions with the greatest amount of deforestation for livestock and row crop agriculture are Central and South America, while commodity crop deforestation was found mainly in Southeast Asia. The region with the greatest forest loss due to shifting agriculture was sub-Saharan Africa.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Agriculture

Further information: [Agricultural expansion](/wiki/Agricultural_expansion "Agricultural expansion")

The overwhelming direct cause of deforestation is agriculture.[[9]](#cite_note-UNFCC-2007-9) [Subsistence farming](/wiki/Subsistence_farming "Subsistence farming") is responsible for 48% of deforestation; [commercial agriculture](/wiki/Commercial_agriculture "Commercial agriculture") is responsible for 32%; [logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging") is responsible for 14%, and fuel wood removals make up 5%.[[9]](#cite_note-UNFCC-2007-9)

More than 80% of deforestation was attributed to agriculture in 2018.[[10]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily-2018-10) Forests are being converted to plantations for coffee, tea, [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil"), rice, [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber"), and various other popular products.[[11]](#cite_note-WWF-2020-11) The rising demand for certain products and global trade arrangements causes [forest conversions](/wiki/Forest_conversion "Forest conversion"), which ultimately leads to [soil erosion](/wiki/Soil_erosion "Soil erosion").[[91]](#cite_note-91) The [top soil](/wiki/Top_soil "Top soil") oftentimes erodes after forests are cleared which leads to sediment increase in rivers and streams.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Anthropogenic_Biomes_of_the_World%2C_Version_2%2C_2000_Global_%2813603947015%29.jpg/250px-Anthropogenic_Biomes_of_the_World%2C_Version_2%2C_2000_Global_%2813603947015%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthropogenic_Biomes_of_the_World,_Version_2,_2000_Global_(13603947015).jpg)

Anthropogenic biomes of the world

Most deforestation also occurs in tropical regions. The estimated amount of total land mass used by agriculture is around 38%.[[92]](#cite_note-Longobardi-2016-92)

Since 1960, roughly 15% of the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") has been removed with the intention of replacing the land with agricultural practices.[[93]](#cite_note-93) It is no coincidence that Brazil has recently become the world's largest beef exporter at the same time that the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") is being clear cut.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Another prevalent method of agricultural deforestation is [slash-and-burn agriculture](/wiki/Slash-and-burn "Slash-and-burn"), which was primarily used by subsistence farmers in tropical regions but has now become increasingly less sustainable. The method does not leave land for continuous agricultural production but instead cuts and burns small plots of forest land which are then converted into agricultural zones. The farmers then exploit the nutrients in the ashes of the burned plants.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) As well as, intentionally set fires can possibly lead to devastating measures when unintentionally spreading fire to more land, which can result in the destruction of the protective canopy.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

The repeated cycle of low yields and shortened fallow periods eventually results in less vegetation being able to grow on once burned lands and a decrease in average soil biomass.[[98]](#cite_note-98) In small local plots sustainability is not an issue because of longer fallow periods and lesser overall deforestation. The relatively small size of the plots allowed for no net input of CO2 to be released.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

#### Livestock ranching

[Consumption and production of beef is the primary driver of deforestation in the Amazon](/wiki/Beef#Environmental_impact "Beef"), with around 80% of all converted land being used to rear cattle.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) 91% of Amazon land deforested since 1970 has been converted to cattle ranching.[[102]](#cite_note-fao-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)

[Livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") [ranching](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch") requires large portions of land to raise herds of animals and livestock crops for consumer needs. According to the [World Wildlife Fund](/wiki/World_Wildlife_Fund "World Wildlife Fund"), "Extensive cattle ranching is the number one culprit of deforestation in virtually every Amazon country, and it accounts for 80% of current deforestation."[[104]](#cite_note-104)

The cattle industry is responsible for a significant amount of [methane emissions](/wiki/Methane_emissions "Methane emissions") since 60% of all mammals on earth are livestock cows.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) Replacing forest land with pastures creates a loss of [forest stock](/wiki/Stocking_(forestry) "Stocking (forestry)"), which leads to the implication of increased greenhouse gas emissions by burning agriculture methodologies and [land-use change](/wiki/Land-use_change "Land-use change").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### Junk Mail

Main article: [Advertising mail](/wiki/Advertising_mail "Advertising mail")

Over 100 million trees per year are cut down for the purpose of junk mail.[[108]](#cite_note-108) A major reason for the United States allowing this deforestation practice is to fund the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service").[[109]](#cite_note-109)

### Wood industry

Further information: [Wood industry](/wiki/Wood_industry "Wood industry")

A large contributing factor to deforestation is the [lumber industry](/wiki/Wood_industry "Wood industry"). A total of almost 4 million hectares (9.9 million acres) of timber,[[110]](#cite_note-auto-110) or about 1.3% of all forest land, is harvested each year. In addition, the increasing demand for low-cost timber products only supports the lumber company to continue logging.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Experts do not agree on whether industrial logging is an important contributor to global deforestation.[[112]](#cite_note-causesof-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) Some argue that poor people are more likely to clear forest because they have no alternatives, others that the poor lack the ability to pay for the materials and labour needed to clear forest.[[112]](#cite_note-causesof-112)

### Economic development

Other causes of contemporary deforestation may include [corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") of government institutions,[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) the [inequitable distribution of wealth and power](/wiki/International_inequality "International inequality"),[[117]](#cite_note-117) [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth")[[118]](#cite_note-population1-118) and [overpopulation](/wiki/Human_overpopulation "Human overpopulation"),[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-r1-120) and [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization").[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) The impact of population growth on deforestation has been contested. One study found that population increases due to high fertility rates were a primary driver of tropical deforestation in only 8% of cases.[[123]](#cite_note-123) In 2000 the United Nations [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO) found that "the role of population dynamics in a local setting may vary from decisive to negligible", and that deforestation can result from "a combination of [population pressure](/wiki/Population_pressure "Population pressure") and stagnating economic, social and technological conditions".[[118]](#cite_note-population1-118)

[Globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") is often viewed as another root cause of deforestation,[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) though there are cases in which the impacts of globalization (new flows of labor, capital, commodities, and ideas) have promoted localized forest recovery.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Garimpo_de_ouro_ilegal_na_Amaz%C3%B4nia_em_Madre_de_Dios%2C_Per%C3%BA.jpg/250px-Garimpo_de_ouro_ilegal_na_Amaz%C3%B4nia_em_Madre_de_Dios%2C_Per%C3%BA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Garimpo_de_ouro_ilegal_na_Amaz%C3%B4nia_em_Madre_de_Dios,_Per%C3%BA.jpg)

[Illegal](/wiki/Illegal_mining "Illegal mining") gold mining in Madre de Dios, [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru").

The degradation of forest ecosystems has also been traced to economic incentives that make forest conversion appear more profitable than forest conservation.[[127]](#cite_note-economicvalue-127) Many important forest functions have no markets, and hence, no economic value that is readily apparent to the forests' owners or the communities that rely on forests for their well-being.[[127]](#cite_note-economicvalue-127)

Some commentators have noted a shift in the drivers of deforestation over the past 30 years.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Whereas deforestation was primarily driven by subsistence activities and government-sponsored development projects like [transmigration](/wiki/Transmigration_program "Transmigration program") in countries like [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [colonization](/wiki/Colonization "Colonization") in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), and so on, during the late 19th century and the first half of the 20th century, by the 1990s the majority of deforestation was caused by industrial factors, including extractive industries, large-scale cattle ranching, and extensive agriculture.[[129]](#cite_note-rud-129) Since 2001, commodity-driven deforestation, which is more likely to be permanent, has accounted for about a quarter of all forest disturbance, and this loss has been concentrated in South America and Southeast Asia.[[130]](#cite_note-CurtisSlay2018-130)

As the human population grows, new homes, communities, and expansions of cities will occur, leading to an increase in roads to connect these communities. Rural roads promote economic development but also facilitate deforestation.[[131]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-131) About 90% of the deforestation has occurred within 100 km of roads in most parts of the Amazon.[[132]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-132)

### Mining

The importance of [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") as a cause of deforestation increased quickly in the beginning the 21st century, among other because of increased demand for minerals. The direct impact of mining is relatively small, but the indirect impacts are much more significant. More than a third of the earth's forests are possibly impacted, at some level and in the years 2001–2021, "755,861 km2... ...had been deforested by causes indirectly related to mining activities alongside other deforestation drivers (based on data from WWF)"[[133]](#cite_note-133)

In the year 2023, mining, including for the elements needed for the [energy transition](/wiki/Energy_transition "Energy transition") strongly contributed to deforestation. Mining is a particular threat to biodiversity: "in 2019, 79 percent of global metal ore extraction originated from five of the six most species-rich biomes".[[134]](#cite_note-134)

### Climate change

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/2001-_Forests_net_absorption_of_greenhouse_gases_-_global.svg/250px-2001-_Forests_net_absorption_of_greenhouse_gases_-_global.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2001-_Forests_net_absorption_of_greenhouse_gases_-_global.svg)

Globally, [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire") and deforestation have reduced forests' net absorption of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas"), reducing their effectiveness at mitigating climate change.[[135]](#cite_note-WRI_Forest_Updake_20250724-135) Global warming increases forest fires that release more greenhouse gases, creating a [feedback loop](/wiki/Positive_feedback "Positive feedback") that causes more warming.[[136]](#cite_note-NYTimes_20250724-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/2002-_Area_of_forest_damage_due_to_fire.svg/250px-2002-_Area_of_forest_damage_due_to_fire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2002-_Area_of_forest_damage_due_to_fire.svg)

Over recent decades, "forest disturbance" (damage) by fire has increased in most of the planet's forest zones.[[137]](#cite_note-PNAS_20250721-137) The increase in area, frequency, and severity of forest fires creates a [positive feedback](/wiki/Positive_feedback "Positive feedback") that increases global warming.[[137]](#cite_note-PNAS_20250721-137)

Another cause of deforestation is due to the [effects of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change"): More [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"),[[138]](#cite_note-138) insect outbreaks, [invasive species](/wiki/Climate_change_and_invasive_species "Climate change and invasive species"), and more frequent [extreme weather](/wiki/Extreme_weather "Extreme weather") events (such as storms) are factors that increase deforestation.[[139]](#cite_note-Seymour-2019-139)

A study suggests that "tropical, arid and temperate forests are experiencing a significant decline in resilience, probably related to increased water limitations and climate variability" which may shift ecosystems towards [critical transitions](/wiki/Critical_transition "Critical transition") and [ecosystem collapses](/wiki/Ecosystem_collapse "Ecosystem collapse").[[140]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41586-022-04959-9-140) By contrast, "boreal forests show divergent local patterns with an average increasing trend in resilience, probably benefiting from warming and CO2 fertilization, which may outweigh the adverse effects of climate change".[[140]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41586-022-04959-9-140) It has been proposed that a loss of resilience in forests "can be detected from the increased temporal autocorrelation (TAC) in the state of the system, reflecting a decline in recovery rates due to the critical slowing down (CSD) of system processes that occur at thresholds".[[140]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41586-022-04959-9-140)

23% of tree cover losses result from wildfires and climate change increase their frequency and power.[[141]](#cite_note-141) The rising temperatures cause massive wildfires especially in the [Boreal forests](/wiki/Boreal_forest "Boreal forest"). One possible effect is the change of the forest composition.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Deforestation can also cause forests to become more fire prone through mechanisms such as logging.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

### Military causes

See also: [Environmental impact of war](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_war "Environmental impact of war")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/US-Huey-helicopter-spraying-Agent-Orange-in-Vietnam.jpg/250px-US-Huey-helicopter-spraying-Agent-Orange-in-Vietnam.jpg)](/wiki/File:US-Huey-helicopter-spraying-Agent-Orange-in-Vietnam.jpg)

[U.S. Army](/wiki/U.S._Army "U.S. Army") [Huey helicopter](/wiki/Bell_UH-1_Iroquois "Bell UH-1 Iroquois") spraying [Agent Orange](/wiki/Agent_Orange "Agent Orange") during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War")

Operations in [war](/wiki/War "War") can also cause deforestation. For example, in the 1945 [Battle of Okinawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa "Battle of Okinawa"), [bombardment](/wiki/Bombardment "Bombardment") and other [combat operations](/wiki/Military_operation "Military operation") reduced a lush tropical landscape into "a vast field of mud, lead, decay and maggots".[[144]](#cite_note-144)

Deforestation can also result from the intentional [tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics") of [military forces](/wiki/Military_forces "Military forces"). [Clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(geography) "Clearing (geography)") forests became an element in the Russian Empire's successful [conquest of the Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasian_War "Caucasian War") in the mid-19th century.[[145]](#cite_note-145)
The British (during the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency")) and the United States (in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War")[[146]](#cite_note-146) and in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War")) used [defoliants](/wiki/Defoliant "Defoliant") (like [Agent Orange](/wiki/Agent_Orange "Agent Orange") or others).[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The destruction of forests in Vietnam War is one of the most commonly used examples of [ecocide](/wiki/Ecocide "Ecocide"), including by Swedish Prime Minister [Olof Palme](/wiki/Olof_Palme "Olof Palme"), lawyers, historians and other academics.[[150]](#cite_note-Zierler-2011-150)[[151]](#cite_note-Falk-1973-151)[[152]](#cite_note-Giovanni-2022-152)

## Impacts

### On atmosphere and climate

Further information: [Deforestation and climate change](/wiki/Deforestation_and_climate_change "Deforestation and climate change")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Biophysical_Effects_on_Global_Temperature_From_Deforestation_by_10%C2%B0_Latitude_Band.jpg/250px-Biophysical_Effects_on_Global_Temperature_From_Deforestation_by_10%C2%B0_Latitude_Band.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biophysical_Effects_on_Global_Temperature_From_Deforestation_by_10%C2%B0_Latitude_Band.jpg)

Biophysical mechanisms by which forests influence climate.[[153]](#cite_note-10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115/full-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Per_capita_CO2_emissions_from_deforestation_for_food_production%2C_OWID.svg/250px-Per_capita_CO2_emissions_from_deforestation_for_food_production%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Per_capita_CO2_emissions_from_deforestation_for_food_production,_OWID.svg)

Per capita CO2 emissions from deforestation for food production

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Manantenina_bushfire.jpg/250px-Manantenina_bushfire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manantenina_bushfire.jpg)

Illegal "[slash-and-burn](/wiki/Slash-and-burn "Slash-and-burn")" practice in [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar"), 2010

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Spatial_pattern_of_forest_carbon_loss_across_the_tropics.webp/250px-Spatial_pattern_of_forest_carbon_loss_across_the_tropics.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Spatial_pattern_of_forest_carbon_loss_across_the_tropics.webp)

Mean annual carbon loss from tropical deforestation.[[154]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41893-022-00854-3-154)

Deforestation is a major contributor to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157) It is often cited as one of the major causes of the enhanced [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect"). Recent calculations suggest that CO2 emissions from deforestation and forest degradation (excluding [peatland](/wiki/Peatland "Peatland") emissions) contribute about 12% of total anthropogenic CO2 emissions, with a range from 6% to 17%.[[158]](#cite_note-158) A 2022 study shows annual carbon emissions from tropical deforestation have doubled during the last two decades and continue to increase: by 0.97 ± 0.16 PgC ([petagrams](/wiki/Petagram "Petagram") of carbon, i.e. billions of tons) per year in 2001–2005 to 1.99 ± 0.13 PgC per year in 2015–2019.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[154]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41893-022-00854-3-154)

According to a review, north of 50°N, large scale deforestation leads to an overall net global cooling; but deforestation in the tropics leads to substantial warming: not just due to CO2 impacts, but also due to other biophysical mechanisms (making carbon-centric metrics inadequate). Moreover, it suggests that standing tropical forests help cool the average global temperature by more than 1 °C.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[153]](#cite_note-10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115/full-153) According to a later study, deforestation in northern latitudes can also increase warming, while the conclusion about cooling from deforestation in these areas made by previous studies results from the failure of models to properly capture the effects of evapotranspiration.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

The incineration and burning of forest plants to clear land releases large amounts of CO2, which contributes to global warming.[[162]](#cite_note-Fearnidel-162) Scientists also state that tropical deforestation releases 1.5 billion tons of carbon each year into the atmosphere.[[163]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-163)

#### Carbon sink or source

See also: [Carbon sequestration](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration"), [Carbon sink](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink"), and [Biomass (energy) § Climate impacts](/wiki/Biomass_(energy)#Climate_impacts "Biomass (energy)")

A study suggests logged and structurally degraded tropical forests are [carbon sources](/wiki/Carbon_cycle "Carbon cycle") for at least a decade – even when recovering[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] – due to larger carbon losses [from soil organic matter](/wiki/Soil_carbon "Soil carbon") and deadwood, indicating that the tropical forest [carbon sink](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink") (at least in South Asia) "may be much smaller than previously estimated", contradicting that "recovering logged and degraded tropical forests are net carbon sinks".[[164]](#cite_note-164)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/SouthEast_Asia_fires_Oct_2006.jpg/250px-SouthEast_Asia_fires_Oct_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:SouthEast_Asia_fires_Oct_2006.jpg)

[Fires on Borneo and Sumatra](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Borneo "Deforestation in Borneo"), 2006. People use [slash-and-burn](/wiki/Slash-and-burn "Slash-and-burn") deforestation to clear land for agriculture.

This section is an excerpt from [Carbon sequestration § Forestry](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration#Forestry "Carbon sequestration").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Carbon_sequestration&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Proportion_of_carbon_stock_in_forest_carbon_pools%2C_2020.svg/250px-Proportion_of_carbon_stock_in_forest_carbon_pools%2C_2020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Proportion_of_carbon_stock_in_forest_carbon_pools,_2020.svg)

Proportion of carbon stock in forest carbon pools, 2020[[165]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_FAO-2020-165)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Total_forest_carbon_stock%2C_by_carbon_pool%2C_2025.svg/250px-Total_forest_carbon_stock%2C_by_carbon_pool%2C_2025.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Total_forest_carbon_stock,_by_carbon_pool,_2025.svg)

Total forest carbon stock, by carbon pool, 2025.

Forests are an important part of the [global carbon cycle](/wiki/Global_carbon_cycle "Global carbon cycle") because trees and plants absorb [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") through [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis"). Therefore, they play an important role in [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation").[[166]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_AR6_WGIII_Ch_13-166): 37 By removing the [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") CO2 from the air, forests function as terrestrial [carbon sinks](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink"), meaning they store large amounts of carbon in the form of biomass, encompassing roots, stems, branches, and leaves. By doing so, forests sequester approximately 25% of human carbon emissions annually, playing a critical role in Earth's climate.[[167]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:15-167) Throughout their lifespan, trees continue to sequester carbon, storing atmospheric CO2 long-term.[[168]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_Sedjo,_R._2012-168) [Sustainable forest management](/wiki/Sustainable_forest_management "Sustainable forest management"), [afforestation](/wiki/Afforestation "Afforestation"), [reforestation](/wiki/Reforestation "Reforestation") are therefore important contributions to climate change mitigation. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An important consideration in such efforts is that forests can turn from sinks to carbon sources.[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170) In 2019 forests took up a third less carbon than they did in the 1990s, due to higher temperatures, [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought")[[171]](#cite_note-171) and deforestation. National-scale forest inventory data also shows trends from 1999 to 2020 that some forests were already approaching climate thresholds shifting them from carbon sinks to carbon sources.[[167]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:15-167) The typical tropical forest may become a carbon source by the 2060s.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

Researchers have found that, in terms of [environmental services](/wiki/Environmental_services "Environmental services"), it is better to avoid deforestation than to allow for deforestation to subsequently reforest, as the latter leads to irreversible effects in terms of [biodiversity loss](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss") and [soil degradation](/wiki/Soil_degradation "Soil degradation").[[173]](#cite_note-173) Furthermore, the probability that legacy carbon will be released from soil is higher in a younger boreal forest.[[174]](#cite_note-174) In particular, boreal forests have been noted to support the growth of Armillaria (honey fungus), which is a root pathogen that breaks down compounds necessary for wood integrity, increasing the likelihood of carbon release.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Global greenhouse gas emissions caused by damage to tropical rainforests may have been substantially underestimated until around 2019.[[176]](#cite_note-176) Additionally, the effects of afforestation and reforestation will be farther in the future than keeping existing forests intact.[[177]](#cite_note-177) It takes much longer − several decades − for the benefits for global warming to manifest to the same carbon sequestration benefits from mature trees in tropical forests and hence from limiting deforestation.[[178]](#cite_note-178) Therefore, scientists consider "the protection and recovery of carbon-rich and long-lived ecosystems, especially natural forests" to be "the major climate [solution](/wiki/Nature-based_solutions "Nature-based solutions")".[[179]](#cite_note-179)

The planting of trees on marginal crop and [pasture](/wiki/Pasture "Pasture") lands helps to incorporate carbon from atmospheric CO  
2 into [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass").[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) For this carbon sequestration process to succeed the carbon must not return to the atmosphere from biomass burning or rotting when the trees die.[[182]](#cite_note-182) Several species of *[Ficus](/wiki/Ficus "Ficus")* such as *Ficus wakefieldii* have been observed to sequester atmospheric CO2 as [calcium oxalate](/wiki/Calcium_oxalate "Calcium oxalate") in the presence of [oxalotrophic](/wiki/Oxalotrophic "Oxalotrophic") bacteria and fungi, which [catabolize](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") the oxalate, which produces calcium carbonate.[[183]](#cite_note-183) The calcium carbonate is precipitated throughout the tree, which also [alkalinizes](/wiki/Alkalinization "Alkalinization") the surrounding soil. These species are current candidates for carbon sequestration in agroforestry. This Calcium-oxalate fixation process was first observed in the *[Iroko](/wiki/Iroko "Iroko")* tree, which can sequester up to a ton of calcium carbonate in the soil over its lifespan. Also Cacti, such as the *[Saguaro](/wiki/Saguaro "Saguaro")*, transfer carbon from the biological cycle to the geological cycle by forming the mineral calcium carbonate.[[184]](#cite_note-184)

Earth offers enough room to plant an additional 0.9 billion ha of tree canopy cover, although this estimate has been criticized,[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) and the true area that has a net cooling effect on the climate when accounting for biophysical feedbacks like albedo is 20-80% lower.[[187]](#cite_note-187)[[188]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:7-188) Planting and protecting these trees would sequester 205 billion tons of carbon if the trees survive future climate stress to reach maturity.[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:7-188) To put this number into perspective, this is about 20 years of current global carbon emissions (as of 2019) .[[190]](#cite_note-190) This level of sequestration would represent about 25% of the atmosphere's carbon pool in 2019.[[188]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:7-188)

Life expectancy of forests varies throughout the world, influenced by tree species, site conditions, and natural disturbance patterns. In some forests, carbon may be stored for centuries, while in other forests, carbon is released with frequent stand replacing fires. Forests that are harvested prior to stand replacing events allow for the retention of carbon in manufactured forest products such as [lumber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber").[[191]](#cite_note-191) However, only a portion of the carbon removed from logged forests ends up as durable goods and buildings. The remainder ends up as sawmill by-products such as pulp, paper, and pallets.[[192]](#cite_note-192) If all new construction globally utilized 90% wood products, largely via adoption of [mass timber](/wiki/Mass_timber "Mass timber") in [low rise](/wiki/Low_rise "Low rise") construction, this could sequester 700 million net tons of carbon per year.[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194) This is in addition to the elimination of carbon emissions from the displaced construction material such as steel or concrete, which are carbon-intense to produce.

A meta-analysis found that mixed species plantations would increase carbon storage alongside other benefits of diversifying planted forests.[[195]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_:6-195)

Although a bamboo forest stores less total carbon than a mature forest of trees, a [bamboo plantation](/wiki/Bamboo_cultivation "Bamboo cultivation") sequesters carbon at a much faster rate than a mature forest or a tree plantation. Therefore, the farming of bamboo timber may have significant carbon sequestration potential.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

The [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO) reported that: "The total carbon stock in forests decreased from 668 gigatonnes in 1990 to 662 gigatonnes in 2020".[[165]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_FAO-2020-165): 11 In [Canada's boreal forests](/wiki/Boreal_forest_of_Canada "Boreal forest of Canada") as much as 80% of the total carbon is stored in the soils as dead organic matter.[[197]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_cfs.nrcan.gc.ca-197)

The [IPCC Sixth Assessment Report](/wiki/IPCC_Sixth_Assessment_Report "IPCC Sixth Assessment Report") says: "Secondary forest regrowth and restoration of degraded forests and non-forest ecosystems can play a large role in carbon sequestration (high confidence) with high resilience to disturbances and additional benefits such as enhanced biodiversity."[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Impacts on temperature are affected by the location of the forest. For example, reforestation in boreal or [subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic "Subarctic") regions has less impact on climate. This is because it substitutes a high-[albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), snow-dominated region with a lower-albedo forest canopy. By contrast, tropical reforestation projects lead to a positive change such as the formation of [clouds](/wiki/Clouds "Clouds"). These clouds then [reflect the sunlight](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), lowering temperatures.[[200]](#cite_note-Carbon_sequestration_Canadell-200): 1457

Planting trees in [tropical climates](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") with [wet seasons](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") has another advantage. In such a setting, trees grow more quickly (fixing more carbon) because they can grow year-round. Trees in tropical climates have, on average, larger, brighter, and more abundant leaves than non-tropical climates. A study of the [girth](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/girth "wikt:girth") of 70,000 trees across [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") has shown that tropical forests fix more carbon dioxide pollution than previously realized. The research suggested almost one-fifth of fossil fuel emissions are absorbed by forests across Africa, [Amazonia](/wiki/Amazonia "Amazonia") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"). Simon Lewis stated, "Tropical forest trees are absorbing about 18% of the carbon dioxide added to the atmosphere each year from burning fossil fuels, substantially buffering the rate of change."[[201]](#cite_note-201)

### On the environment

According to a 2020 study, if deforestation continues at current rates it can trigger a total or almost total [extinction of humanity](/wiki/Human_extinction "Human extinction") in the next 20 to 40 years. They conclude that "from a statistical point of view... the probability that our civilisation survives itself is less than 10% in the most optimistic scenario." To avoid this collapse, humanity should pass from a civilization dominated by the economy to "cultural society" that "privileges the interest of the ecosystem above the individual interest of its components, but eventually in accordance with the overall communal interest."[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203)

#### Changes to the water cycle

The [water cycle](/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle") is also affected by deforestation. Trees extract [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") through their roots and release it into the atmosphere. When part of a forest is removed, the trees no longer transpire this water, resulting in a much [drier climate](/wiki/Dry_climate "Dry climate"). Deforestation reduces the content of water in the soil and groundwater as well as atmospheric moisture. The dry soil leads to lower water intake for the trees to extract.[[204]](#cite_note-204) Deforestation reduces soil cohesion, so that [erosion](/wiki/Soil_erosion "Soil erosion"), flooding and [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide") ensue.[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206)

Shrinking [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") lessens the landscape's capacity to intercept, retain and [transpire](/wiki/Transpiration "Transpiration") precipitation. Instead of trapping precipitation, which then percolates to groundwater systems, deforested areas become sources of surface water runoff, which moves much faster than subsurface flows. Forests return most of the water that falls as precipitation to the atmosphere by transpiration. In contrast, when an area is deforested, almost all precipitation is lost as run-off.[[207]](#cite_note-207) That quicker transport of surface water can translate into [flash flooding](/wiki/Flash_flood "Flash flood") and more localized floods than would occur with the forest cover. Deforestation also contributes to decreased [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration"), which lessens atmospheric moisture which in some cases affects precipitation levels downwind from the deforested area, as water is not recycled to downwind forests, but is lost in runoff and returns directly to the oceans. According to one study, in deforested north and northwest China, the average annual precipitation decreased by one third between the 1950s and the 1980s.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Madagascar_highland_plateau.jpg/250px-Madagascar_highland_plateau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madagascar_highland_plateau.jpg)

Deforestation of the [Highland Plateau](/wiki/Central_Highlands_(Madagascar) "Central Highlands (Madagascar)") in Madagascar has led to extensive [siltation](/wiki/Siltation "Siltation") and unstable flows of western rivers.

Trees, and plants in general, affect the [water cycle](/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle") significantly:[[209]](#cite_note-Mishra-2010-209)

* their canopies intercept a proportion of [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)"), which is then evaporated back to the atmosphere ([canopy interception](/wiki/Interception_(water) "Interception (water)"));
* their litter, stems and trunks slow down [surface runoff](/wiki/Surface_runoff "Surface runoff");
* their roots create [macropores](/wiki/Macropore "Macropore") – large conduits – in the soil that increase [infiltration](/wiki/Infiltration_(hydrology) "Infiltration (hydrology)") of water;
* they contribute to terrestrial evaporation and reduce [soil moisture](/wiki/Water_content "Water content") via [transpiration](/wiki/Transpiration "Transpiration");
* their [litter](/wiki/Plant_litter "Plant litter") and other organic residue change soil properties that affect the capacity of soil to store water.
* their leaves control the [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity") of the atmosphere by [transpiring](/wiki/Transpiration "Transpiration"). 99% of the water absorbed by the roots moves up to the leaves and is transpired.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

As a result, the presence or absence of trees can change the quantity of water on the surface, in the soil or groundwater, or in the atmosphere. This in turn changes erosion rates and the availability of water for either ecosystem functions or human services. Deforestation on lowland plains moves cloud formation and rainfall to higher elevations.[[211]](#cite_note-211)

The forest may have little impact on flooding in the case of large rainfall events, which overwhelm the storage capacity of forest soil if the soils are at or close to saturation.

[Tropical rainforests](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest") produce about 30% of Earth's [fresh water](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water").[[212]](#cite_note-The_Times-212)

Deforestation disrupts normal weather patterns creating hotter and drier weather thus increasing drought, desertification, crop failures, melting of the polar ice caps, [coastal flooding](/wiki/Coastal_flooding "Coastal flooding") and displacement of major vegetation regimes.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

#### Soil erosion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Erp_-_20111006_%286%29.jpg/250px-Erp_-_20111006_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erp_-_20111006_(6).jpg)

Deforestation in [France](/wiki/France "France").

Due to surface [plant litter](/wiki/Plant_litter "Plant litter"), forests that are undisturbed have a minimal rate of [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"). The rate of erosion occurs from deforestation, because it decreases the amount of litter cover, which provides protection from [surface runoff](/wiki/Surface_runoff "Surface runoff").[[214]](#cite_note-214) The rate of erosion is around 2 metric tons per square kilometre.[[215]](#cite_note-Homestead-215)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] This can be an advantage in excessively leached tropical rain forest soils. Forestry operations themselves also increase erosion through the [development of](/wiki/Road_building "Road building") ([forest](/wiki/Forest_road "Forest road")) [roads](/wiki/Road "Road") and the use of mechanized equipment.[[77]](#cite_note-IPCC_AR6-77)

Deforestation in China's Loess Plateau many years ago has led to soil erosion; this erosion has led to valleys opening up. The increase of soil in the runoff causes the Yellow River to flood and makes it yellow-colored.[[215]](#cite_note-Homestead-215)

Greater erosion is not always a consequence of deforestation, as observed in the southwestern regions of the US. In these areas, the loss of grass due to the presence of trees and other shrubbery leads to more erosion than when trees are removed.[[215]](#cite_note-Homestead-215)

Soils are reinforced by the presence of trees, which secure the soil by binding their roots to soil bedrock. Due to deforestation, the removal of trees causes sloped lands to be more susceptible to [landslides.](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide")[[209]](#cite_note-Mishra-2010-209)

#### Other changes to the soil

Clearing forests changes the environment of the [microbial communities within the soil](/wiki/Soil_biology "Soil biology"), and causes a [loss of biodiversity](/wiki/Loss_of_biodiversity "Loss of biodiversity") in regards to the microbes since biodiversity is actually highly dependent on [soil texture](/wiki/Soil_texture "Soil texture").[[216]](#cite_note-YaleNews-2014-216) Although the effect of deforestation has much more profound consequences on sandier soils compared to clay-like soils, the disruptions caused by deforestation ultimately reduces properties of soil such as [hydraulic conductivity](/wiki/Hydraulic_conductivity "Hydraulic conductivity") and water storage, thus reducing the efficiency of water and heat absorption.[[216]](#cite_note-YaleNews-2014-216)[[217]](#cite_note-Shukla-1990-217) In a simulation of the deforestation process in the Amazon, researchers found that surface and soil temperatures increased by 1 to 3 degrees Celsius demonstrating the loss of the soil's ability to absorb radiation and moisture.[[217]](#cite_note-Shukla-1990-217) Furthermore, soils that are rich in organic decay matter are more susceptible to fire, especially during long droughts.[[216]](#cite_note-YaleNews-2014-216)

Changes in soil properties could turn the soil itself into a [carbon source](/wiki/Carbon_source "Carbon source") rather than a [carbon sink](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink").[[218]](#cite_note-Rebecca-2007-218)

#### Biodiversity loss

Further information: [Biodiversity loss](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss")

Deforestation on a human scale results in decline in [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"),[[219]](#cite_note-219) and on a natural global scale is known to cause the extinction of many species.[[220]](#cite_note-Benton-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221) The removal or destruction of areas of forest cover has resulted in a degraded environment with reduced [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity").[[120]](#cite_note-r1-120) Forests support biodiversity, providing habitat for [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife");[[222]](#cite_note-222) moreover, forests foster [medicinal conservation](/wiki/Medicinal_plants "Medicinal plants").[[223]](#cite_note-223) With forest [biotopes](/wiki/Biotope "Biotope") being irreplaceable source of new drugs (such as [taxol](/wiki/Paclitaxel "Paclitaxel")), deforestation can destroy [genetic](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") variations (such as crop resistance) irretrievably.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Illegal_export_of_rosewood_001.jpg/250px-Illegal_export_of_rosewood_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illegal_export_of_rosewood_001.jpg)

[Illegal logging in Madagascar](/wiki/Illegal_logging_in_Madagascar "Illegal logging in Madagascar"). In 2009, the vast majority of the illegally obtained [rosewood](/wiki/Rosewood "Rosewood") was exported to [China](/wiki/China "China").

Since the tropical rainforests are the most diverse [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") on Earth[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226) and about 80% of the world's known [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") can be found in tropical rainforests,[[227]](#cite_note-227) removal or destruction of significant areas of forest cover has resulted in a [degraded](/wiki/Soil_degradation "Soil degradation")[[228]](#cite_note-228) environment with reduced biodiversity.[[220]](#cite_note-Benton-220)[[229]](#cite_note-229) Road construction and development of adjacent land, which greatly reduces the area of intact wilderness and causes soil erosion, is a major contributing factor to the loss of biodiversity in tropical regions.[[77]](#cite_note-IPCC_AR6-77) A study in [Rondônia](/wiki/Rond%C3%B4nia "Rondônia"), Brazil, has shown that deforestation also removes the microbial community which is involved in the recycling of nutrients, the production of clean water and the removal of pollutants.[[230]](#cite_note-ro-230)

It has been estimated that 137 plant, animal and insect species go extinct every day due to rainforest deforestation, which equates to 50,000 species a year.[[231]](#cite_note-rain-tree.com-231) Others state that tropical rainforest deforestation is contributing to the ongoing [Holocene mass extinction](/wiki/Holocene_mass_extinction "Holocene mass extinction").[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233) The known extinction rates from deforestation rates are very low, approximately one species per year from mammals and birds, which extrapolates to approximately 23,000 species per year for all species. Predictions have been made that more than 40% of the animal and [plant species](/wiki/Flora "Flora") in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") could be wiped out in the 21st century.[[234]](#cite_note-234) Such predictions were called into question by 1995 data that show that within regions of Southeast Asia much of the original forest has been converted to monospecific plantations, but that potentially endangered species are few and tree flora remains widespread and stable.[[235]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-235)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Rain_forest_location_map.png/330px-Rain_forest_location_map.png)](/wiki/File:Rain_forest_location_map.png)

World map of [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest")

[Tropical rainforest](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest")

[Temperate/subtropical rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest")

Scientific understanding of the process of extinction is insufficient to accurately make predictions about the impact of deforestation on biodiversity.[[236]](#cite_note-236) Most predictions of forestry related biodiversity loss are based on species-area models, with an underlying assumption that as the forest declines species diversity will decline similarly.[[237]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-237) However, many such models have been proven to be wrong and [loss of habitat](/wiki/Loss_of_habitat "Loss of habitat") does not necessarily lead to large scale loss of species.[[237]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-237) Species-area models are known to overpredict the number of species known to be threatened in areas where actual deforestation is ongoing, and greatly overpredict the number of threatened species that are widespread.[[235]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-235)

In 2012, a study of the Brazilian Amazon predicts that despite a lack of extinctions thus far, up to 90 percent of predicted extinctions will finally occur in the next 40 years.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

#### Oxygen-supply misconception

Rainforests are widely believed by lay people to provide a significant amount of the world's [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen").[[212]](#cite_note-The_Times-212) However, scientific research has found that rainforests contribute little net oxygen to the [Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere"), so deforestation has only a minor effect on atmospheric oxygen levels.[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240) In fact, about 50 percent of the Earth's oxygen is produced by [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"), mostly in the oceans.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

### On human health

Deforestation reduces safe working hours for millions of people in the tropics, especially for those performing heavy labour outdoors. Continued global heating and forest loss is expected to amplify these impacts, reducing work hours for vulnerable groups even more.[[242]](#cite_note-242) A study conducted from 2002 to 2018 also determined that the increase in temperature as a result of climate change, and the lack of shade due to deforestation, has increased the [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") of workers in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[243]](#cite_note-Wolff-2021-243) A 2025 pan-tropical analysis estimated that local warming from tropical deforestation (2001–2020) exposed ~345 million people and was associated with ~28,330 additional heat-related deaths per year, accounting for roughly one-third of heat-attributable mortality in areas of forest loss, with the highest rates in Southeast Asia.[[244]](#cite_note-244)

#### Infectious diseases

Deforestation eliminates a great number of species of plants and animals which also often results in exposure of people to [zoonotic diseases](/wiki/Zoonosis "Zoonosis").[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)[[245]](#cite_note-245)[[246]](#cite_note-246) Forest-associated diseases include malaria, Chagas disease (also known as American trypanosomiasis), African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness), leishmaniasis, Lyme disease, HIV and Ebola.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12) The majority of new infectious diseases affecting humans, including the [SARS-CoV-2 virus](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2 "SARS-CoV-2") that caused the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), are zoonotic and their emergence may be linked to habitat loss due to forest area change and the expansion of human populations into forest areas, which both increase human exposure to wildlife.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)

Deforestation has been coupled with an increase in the occurrence of disease outbreaks. In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), thousands of acres of forest have been cleared for pig farms. This has resulted in an increase in the spread of the [Nipah virus](/wiki/Nipah_virus "Nipah virus").[[247]](#cite_note-247)[[248]](#cite_note-248) In [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), deforestation has led to an increase in malaria cases which is now the leading cause of morbidity and mortality the country.[[249]](#cite_note-249)[[250]](#cite_note-250) A 2017 study found that deforestation substantially increased the incidence of malaria in Nigeria.[[251]](#cite_note-251)

Another pathway through which deforestation affects disease is the relocation and dispersion of disease-carrying hosts. This disease emergence pathway can be called "[range expansion](/wiki/Colonisation_(biology) "Colonisation (biology)")", whereby the host's range (and thereby the range of pathogens) expands to new geographic areas.[[252]](#cite_note-252) Through deforestation, hosts and reservoir species are forced into neighboring habitats. Accompanying the reservoir species are pathogens that have the ability to find new hosts in previously unexposed regions. As these pathogens and species come into closer contact with humans, they are infected both directly and indirectly. Another example of range expansion due to deforestation and other [anthropogenic](/wiki/Anthropogenic_hazard "Anthropogenic hazard") habitat impacts includes the [Capybara](/wiki/Capybara "Capybara") rodent in [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay").[[253]](#cite_note-253)

According to the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum"), 31% of [emerging diseases](/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease "Emerging infectious disease") are linked to deforestation.[[254]](#cite_note-254) A publication by the [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme") in 2016 found that deforestation, [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), and [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") agriculture are among the main causes that increase the risk of [zoonotic diseases](/wiki/Zoonosis "Zoonosis"), that is diseases that pass from animals to humans.[[255]](#cite_note-UNEP-2016-255)

##### COVID-19 pandemic

See also: [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and [Pandemic prevention § Environmental policy and economics](/wiki/Pandemic_prevention#Environmental_policy_and_economics "Pandemic prevention")

Scientists have linked the [Coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019") to the destruction of nature, especially to deforestation, [habitat loss](/wiki/Habitat_loss "Habitat loss") in general and [wildlife trade](/wiki/Wildlife_trade "Wildlife trade").[[256]](#cite_note-256) According to the [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme") (UNEP) the [Coronavirus disease 2019](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019") is zoonotic, e.g., the virus passed from animals to humans. UNEP concludes that: "The most fundamental way to protect ourselves from zoonotic diseases is to prevent destruction of nature. Where ecosystems are healthy and biodiverse, they are resilient, adaptable and help to regulate diseases.[[257]](#cite_note-257)

### On the economy and agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Malayasia_iko_2002169.jpg/250px-Malayasia_iko_2002169.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malayasia_iko_2002169.jpg)

A satellite image showing [deforestation for a palm oil plantation](/wiki/Social_and_environmental_impact_of_palm_oil "Social and environmental impact of palm oil") in Malaysia

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. The reason given is: **cites are very old**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(June 2020)* |

Economic losses due to [deforestation in Brazil](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Brazil "Deforestation in Brazil") could reach around 317 billion dollars per year, approximately 7 times higher in comparison to the cost of all commodities produced through deforestation.[[258]](#cite_note-TheWorldBank-258)

The forest products industry is a large part of the economy in both developed and developing countries. Short-term economic gains made by conversion of forest to agriculture, or [over-exploitation](/wiki/Over-exploitation "Over-exploitation") of wood products, typically leads to a loss of long-term income and long-term biological productivity. [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar"), [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and many other regions have experienced lower revenue because of declining timber harvests. Illegal logging causes billions of dollars of losses to national economies annually.[[259]](#cite_note-259)

The resilience of human food systems and their capacity to adapt to future change is linked to biodiversity – including dryland-adapted shrub and tree species that help combat desertification, forest-dwelling insects, bats and bird species that pollinate crops, trees with extensive root systems in [mountain ecosystems](/wiki/Mountain_ecosystems "Mountain ecosystems") that prevent [soil erosion](/wiki/Soil_erosion "Soil erosion"), and mangrove species that provide resilience against flooding in coastal areas.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12) With climate change exacerbating the risks to food systems, the role of forests in capturing and storing carbon and mitigating climate change is important for the agricultural sector.[[12]](#cite_note-FAO_&_UNEP-2020-12)
