## Monitoring

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Terra_Ind%C3%ADgena_Tenharim_do_Igarap%C3%A9_Preto%2C_Amazonas_%2841737918674%29.jpg/250px-Terra_Ind%C3%ADgena_Tenharim_do_Igarap%C3%A9_Preto%2C_Amazonas_%2841737918674%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terra_Ind%C3%ADgena_Tenharim_do_Igarap%C3%A9_Preto,_Amazonas_(41737918674).jpg)

Agents from IBAMA, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")'s environmental police, searching for illegal logging activity in [Indigenous territory](/wiki/Indigenous_territory_(Brazil) "Indigenous territory (Brazil)") in the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest"), 2018

There are multiple methods that are appropriate and reliable for reducing and monitoring deforestation. One method is the "visual interpretation of aerial photos or satellite imagery that is labor-intensive but does not require high-level training in computer image processing or extensive computational resources".[[132]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-132) Another method includes hot-spot analysis (that is, locations of rapid change) using expert opinion or coarse resolution satellite data to identify locations for detailed digital analysis with high resolution satellite images.[[132]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-132) Deforestation is typically assessed by quantifying the amount of area deforested, measured at the present time.
From an environmental point of view, quantifying the damage and its possible consequences is a more important task, while conservation efforts are more focused on forested land protection and development of land-use alternatives to avoid continued deforestation.[[132]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-132) Deforestation rate and total area deforested have been widely used for monitoring deforestation in many regions, including the Brazilian Amazon deforestation monitoring by INPE.[[163]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-163) A global satellite view is available, an example of [land change science](/wiki/Land_change_science "Land change science") monitoring of land cover over time.[[260]](#cite_note-260)[[261]](#cite_note-261)

Satellite imaging has become crucial in obtaining data on levels of deforestation and reforestation. [Landsat](/wiki/Landsat "Landsat") [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") data, for example, has been used to map tropical deforestation as part of [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")'s Landsat *Pathfinder Humid Tropical Deforestation Project*. The project yielded deforestation maps for the [Amazon Basin](/wiki/Amazon_Basin "Amazon Basin"), [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"), and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") for three periods in the 1970s, 1980s, and 1990s.[[262]](#cite_note-262)

Greenpeace has mapped out the forests that are still intact[[263]](#cite_note-263) and published this information on the internet.[[264]](#cite_note-264) [World Resources Institute](/wiki/World_Resources_Institute "World Resources Institute") in turn has made a simpler thematic map[[265]](#cite_note-265) showing the amount of forests present just before the age of man (8000 years ago) and the current (reduced) levels of forest.[[266]](#cite_note-266)

## Control

### International, national and subnational policies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Conceptual_framework_of_a_policy_mix_perspective_on_zero-deforestation_governance.png/250px-Conceptual_framework_of_a_policy_mix_perspective_on_zero-deforestation_governance.png)](/wiki/File:Conceptual_framework_of_a_policy_mix_perspective_on_zero-deforestation_governance.png)

An incomplete concept of a framework of policy mix sequencing for zero-deforestation governance. Non-intervention in processes related to beef production via policies may be a main driver of tropical deforestation.

Further information: [Sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") and [Universal basic income in Brazil](/wiki/Universal_basic_income_in_Brazil "Universal basic income in Brazil")

Policies for forest protection include information and education programs, economic measures to increase revenue returns from authorized activities and measures to increase effectiveness of "forest technicians and forest managers".[[267]](#cite_note-267) Poverty and agricultural rent were found to be principal factors leading to deforestation.[[268]](#cite_note-268) Contemporary domestic and foreign political decision-makers could possibly create and implement policies whose outcomes ensure that economic activities in critical forests are consistent with their scientifically ascribed value for [ecosystem services](/wiki/Ecosystem_service "Ecosystem service"), climate change mitigation and other purposes.

Such policies may use and organize the development of complementary technical and economic means – including for lower levels of beef production, sales and consumption (which would also have major benefits for [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation")),[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270) higher levels of specified other economic activities in such areas (such as reforestation, forest protection, [sustainable agriculture](/wiki/Sustainable_agriculture "Sustainable agriculture") for specific classes of food products and [quaternary work](/wiki/Quaternary_sector_of_the_economy "Quaternary sector of the economy") in general), [product information](/wiki/Product_information "Product information") [requirements](/wiki/Law-making "Law-making"), practice- and product-certifications and [eco-tariffs](/wiki/Eco-tariffs "Eco-tariffs"), along with the required monitoring and [traceability](/wiki/Traceability "Traceability"). [Inducing the creation and enforcement of such policies](/wiki/Policy#Induction_of_policies "Policy") could, for instance, achieve a global phase-out of [deforestation-associated beef](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_beef "Environmental impact of beef").[[271]](#cite_note-271)[[272]](#cite_note-272)[[273]](#cite_note-273)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With complex polycentric governance measures, goals like sufficient climate change mitigation as decided with e.g. the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") and a stoppage of deforestation by 2030 as decided at the [2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference](/wiki/2021_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference") could be achieved.[[274]](#cite_note-10.1017/sus.2021.21-274) A study has suggested higher income nations need to reduce [imports](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") of tropical forest-related products and help with theoretically forest-related socioeconomic development. Proactive government policies and international forest policies "revisit[ing] and redesign[ing] global forest trade" are needed as well.[[275]](#cite_note-275)[[276]](#cite_note-276)

In 2022 the [European parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") approved a bill aiming to stop the import linked with deforestation. This [EU Regulation on Deforestation-free products](/wiki/EU_Regulation_on_Deforestation-free_products "EU Regulation on Deforestation-free products") (EUDR), may cause to Brazil, for example, to stop deforestation for agricultural production and begun to "increase productivity on existing agricultural land".[[277]](#cite_note-277) The legislation was adopted with some changes by the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") in May 2023 and is expected to enter into force several weeks after. The bill requires companies who want to import certain types of products to the European Union to prove the production of those commodities is not linked to areas deforested after 31 of December 2020. It prohibits also import of products linked with [Human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") abuse. The list of products includes: [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil"), [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle"), [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood"), [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), [cocoa](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean"), [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") and [soy](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean"). Some derivatives of those products are also included: [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate"), [furniture](/wiki/Furniture "Furniture"), [printed paper](/wiki/Paper "Paper") and several palm oil based derivates.[[278]](#cite_note-278)[[279]](#cite_note-279)

But unfortunately, as the report *Bankrolling ecosystem destruction* shows,[[280]](#cite_note-280) this regulation of product imports is not enough. The European financial sector is investing billions of euros in the destruction of nature. Banks do not respond positively to requests to stop this, which is why the report calls for European regulation in this area to be tightened and for banks to be banned from continuing to finance deforestation.[[281]](#cite_note-281)

### International pledges

In 2014, about 40 countries signed the [New York Declaration on Forests](/wiki/New_York_Declaration_on_Forests "New York Declaration on Forests"), a voluntary pledge to halve deforestation by 2020 and end it by 2030. The agreement was not legally binding, however, and some key countries, such as Brazil, China, and Russia, did not sign onto it. As a result, the effort failed, and deforestation increased from 2014 to 2020.[[282]](#cite_note-282)[[283]](#cite_note-Butlerb-283)

In November 2021, 141 countries (with around 85% of the world's [primary](/wiki/Primary_forest "Primary forest") [tropical forests](/wiki/Tropical_forest "Tropical forest") and 90% of global [tree cover](/wiki/Tree_cover "Tree cover")) agreed at the [COP26 climate summit in Glasgow](/wiki/2021_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference") to the *Glasgow Leaders' Declaration on Forests and Land Use*, a pledge to end and reverse deforestation by 2030.[[283]](#cite_note-Butlerb-283)[[284]](#cite_note-SpringJessop-284)[[285]](#cite_note-285) The agreement was accompanied by about $19.2 billion in associated funding commitments.[[284]](#cite_note-SpringJessop-284)

The 2021 [Glasgow agreement](/wiki/Glasgow_Agreement "Glasgow Agreement") improved on the New York Declaration by now including Brazil and many other countries that did not sign the 2014 agreement.[[283]](#cite_note-Butlerb-283)[[284]](#cite_note-SpringJessop-284) Some key nations with high rates of deforestation (including Malaysia, Cambodia, Laos, Paraguay, and Myanmar) have not signed the Glasgow Declaration.[[284]](#cite_note-SpringJessop-284) Like the earlier agreement, the Glasgow Leaders' Declaration was entered into outside the [UN Framework Convention on Climate Change](/wiki/United_Nations_Framework_Convention_on_Climate_Change "United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change") and is thus not legally binding.[[284]](#cite_note-SpringJessop-284)

In November 2021, the EU executive outlined a draft law requiring companies to prove that the agricultural commodities beef, wood, palm oil, soy, [coffee](/wiki/Coffee_bean "Coffee bean") and [cocoa](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean") destined for the EU's 450 million consumers were not linked to deforestation.[[286]](#cite_note-286) In September 2022, the EU Parliament supported and strengthened the plan from the EU's executive with 453 votes to 57.[[287]](#cite_note-287)

In 2018 the biggest palm oil trader, Wilmar, decided to control its suppliers to avoid deforestation[[288]](#cite_note-288)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2021, over 100 world leaders, representing countries containing more than 85% of the world's forests, committed to halt and reverse deforestation and land degradation by 2030.[[289]](#cite_note-289)

### Land rights

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Shennongjia_virgin_forest.jpg/250px-Shennongjia_virgin_forest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shennongjia_virgin_forest.jpg)

Transferring [land rights](/wiki/Land_rights "Land rights") to indigenous inhabitants is argued to efficiently conserve forests.

Indigenous communities have long been the frontline of resistance against deforestation.[[290]](#cite_note-290) Transferring rights over land from public domain to its [indigenous inhabitants](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") is argued to be a cost-effective strategy to conserve forests.[[291]](#cite_note-Guardian1-291) This includes the protection of such rights entitled in existing laws, such as India's [Forest Rights Act](/wiki/The_Scheduled_Tribes_and_Other_Traditional_Forest_Dwellers_(Recognition_of_Forest_Rights)_Act,_2006 "The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006").[[291]](#cite_note-Guardian1-291) The transferring of such rights in [China](/wiki/China "China"), perhaps the largest [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") in modern times, has been argued to have increased forest cover.[[292]](#cite_note-rightsandresources-292) In [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), forested areas given tenure to indigenous groups have even lower rates of [clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(geography) "Clearing (geography)") than [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park").[[292]](#cite_note-rightsandresources-292)

Community concessions in the [Congolian rainforests](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests") have significantly less deforestation as communities are incentivized to manage the land sustainably, even reducing poverty.[[293]](#cite_note-293)

### Forest management

Further information: [Forest management](/wiki/Forest_management "Forest management")

In areas where "[slash-and-burn](/wiki/Slash-and-burn "Slash-and-burn")" is practiced, switching to "[slash-and-char](/wiki/Slash-and-char "Slash-and-char")" would prevent the rapid deforestation and subsequent degradation of soils. The [biochar](/wiki/Biochar "Biochar") thus created, given back to the soil, is not only a durable [carbon sequestration](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration") method, but it also is an extremely beneficial [amendment](/wiki/Soil_conditioner "Soil conditioner") to the soil. Mixed with [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") it brings the creation of [terra preta](/wiki/Terra_preta "Terra preta"), one of the richest soils on the planet and the only one known to regenerate itself.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Bamboo_Feb09.jpg/250px-Bamboo_Feb09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bamboo_Feb09.jpg)

Bamboo is advocated as a more sustainable alternative for cutting down wood for fuel.[[294]](#cite_note-bamboo-294)

Certification, as provided by global certification systems such as [Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification](/wiki/Programme_for_the_Endorsement_of_Forest_Certification "Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification") and [Forest Stewardship Council](/wiki/Forest_Stewardship_Council "Forest Stewardship Council"), contributes to tackling deforestation by creating market demand for timber from sustainably managed forests. According to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), "A major condition for the adoption of sustainable forest management is a demand for products that are produced sustainably and consumer willingness to pay for the higher costs entailed. [...] By promoting the positive attributes of forest products from sustainably managed forests, certification focuses on the demand side of environmental conservation."[[295]](#cite_note-295)

Brazil’s [Amazon soy moratorium](/wiki/Amazon_soy_moratorium "Amazon soy moratorium"), an agreement among commodities traders to not purchase from areas deforested after 2008, has contributed to the reduction in deforestion rates and less than 2% of the production land was non-compliant in 2018/19.[[296]](#cite_note-296)

### Financial compensations for reducing emissions from deforestation

Main article: [Reducing emissions from deforestation and forest degradation](/wiki/Reducing_emissions_from_deforestation_and_forest_degradation "Reducing emissions from deforestation and forest degradation")

[Reducing emissions from deforestation and forest degradation (REDD)](/wiki/Reducing_emissions_from_deforestation_and_forest_degradation "Reducing emissions from deforestation and forest degradation") in developing countries has emerged as a new potential to complement ongoing climate policies. The idea consists in providing financial compensations for the reduction of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from deforestation and forest degradation".[[297]](#cite_note-297) REDD can be seen as an alternative to the [emissions trading](/wiki/Emissions_trading "Emissions trading") system as in the latter, polluters must pay for permits for the right to emit certain pollutants (i.e. CO2).

Main international organizations including the United Nations and the World Bank, have begun to develop programs aimed at curbing deforestation. The blanket term *Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation* (REDD) describes these sorts of programs, which use direct monetary or other incentives to encourage developing countries to limit and/or roll back deforestation. Funding has been an issue, but at the [UN Framework Convention on Climate Change](/wiki/UN_Framework_Convention_on_Climate_Change "UN Framework Convention on Climate Change") (UNFCCC) Conference of the Parties-15 (COP-15) in Copenhagen in December 2009, an accord was reached with a collective commitment by developed countries for new and additional resources, including [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and investments through international institutions, that will approach US$30 billion for the period 2010–2012.[[298]](#cite_note-298)

Significant work is underway on tools for use in monitoring developing countries' adherence to their agreed REDD targets. These tools, which rely on remote forest monitoring using satellite imagery and other data sources, include the [Center for Global Development](/wiki/Center_for_Global_Development "Center for Global Development")'s FORMA (Forest Monitoring for Action) initiative[[299]](#cite_note-299) and the [Group on Earth Observations](/wiki/Group_on_Earth_Observations "Group on Earth Observations")' Forest Carbon Tracking Portal.[[300]](#cite_note-300) Methodological guidance for forest monitoring was also emphasized at COP-15.[[301]](#cite_note-301) The environmental organization [Avoided Deforestation Partners](/wiki/Avoided_Deforestation_Partners "Avoided Deforestation Partners") leads the campaign for development of REDD through funding from the U.S. government.[[302]](#cite_note-302)

## History

Further information: [Timeline of environmental history](/wiki/Timeline_of_environmental_history "Timeline of environmental history")

### Prehistory

The [Carboniferous Rainforest Collapse](/wiki/Carboniferous_Rainforest_Collapse "Carboniferous Rainforest Collapse")[[220]](#cite_note-Benton-220) was an event that occurred 300 million years ago. Climate change devastated tropical rainforests causing the extinction of many plant and animal species. The change was abrupt, specifically, at this time climate became cooler and drier, conditions that are not favorable to the growth of rainforests and much of the biodiversity within them. Rainforests were fragmented forming shrinking 'islands' further and further apart. Populations such as the sub class [Lissamphibia](/wiki/Lissamphibia "Lissamphibia") were devastated, whereas [Reptilia](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile") survived the collapse. The surviving organisms were better adapted to the drier environment left behind and served as legacies in succession after the collapse.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg/250px-N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)](/wiki/File:N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)

An array of Neolithic artifacts, including bracelets, ax heads, chisels, and polishing tools.

Rainforests once covered 14% of the earth's land surface; now they cover a mere 6% and experts estimate that the last remaining rainforests could be consumed in less than 40 years.[[303]](#cite_note-303)
Small scale deforestation was practiced by some societies for tens of thousands of years before the beginnings of civilization.[[304]](#cite_note-FutureEaters-304) The first evidence of deforestation appears in the [Mesolithic period](/wiki/Mesolithic_period "Mesolithic period").[[305]](#cite_note-305) It was probably used to convert closed forests into more open ecosystems favourable to game animals.[[304]](#cite_note-FutureEaters-304) With the advent of agriculture, larger areas began to be deforested, and fire became the prime tool to clear land for crops. In Europe there is little solid evidence before 7000 BC. Mesolithic [foragers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") used fire to create openings for [red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer") and [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"). In Great Britain, shade-tolerant species such as [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") and [ash](/wiki/Ash_tree "Ash tree") are replaced in the [pollen record](/wiki/Palynology "Palynology") by [hazels](/wiki/Hazel "Hazel"), brambles, grasses and nettles. Removal of the forests led to decreased [transpiration](/wiki/Transpiration "Transpiration"), resulting in the formation of upland [peat bogs](/wiki/Peat_bog "Peat bog"). Widespread decrease in [elm](/wiki/Elm "Elm") [pollen](/wiki/Pollen "Pollen") across Europe between 8400 and 8300 BC and 7200–7000 BC, starting in southern Europe and gradually moving north to Great Britain, may represent land [clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(geography) "Clearing (geography)") by fire at the onset of [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") agriculture.

The [Neolithic period](/wiki/Neolithic_period "Neolithic period") saw extensive deforestation for [farming land](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture").[[306]](#cite_note-306)[[307]](#cite_note-307) Stone axes were being made from about 3000 BC not just from flint, but from a wide variety of hard rocks from across Britain and North America as well. They include the noted [Langdale axe industry](/wiki/Langdale_axe_industry "Langdale axe industry") in the [English Lake District](/wiki/English_Lake_District "English Lake District"), quarries developed at [Penmaenmawr](/wiki/Penmaenmawr "Penmaenmawr") in [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales") and numerous other locations. Rough-outs were made locally near the quarries, and some were polished locally to give a fine finish. This step not only increased the [mechanical strength](/wiki/Mechanical_strength "Mechanical strength") of the axe, but also made penetration of wood easier. [Flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") was still used from sources such as [Grimes Graves](/wiki/Grimes_Graves "Grimes Graves") but from many other mines across Europe.

Evidence of deforestation has been found in [Minoan](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"); for example the environs of the [Palace of Knossos](/wiki/Palace_of_Knossos "Palace of Knossos") were severely deforested in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[308]](#cite_note-308)

### Pre-industrial history

See also: [Deforestation during the Roman period](/wiki/Deforestation_during_the_Roman_period "Deforestation during the Roman period") and [Deforestation by continent](/wiki/Deforestation_by_continent "Deforestation by continent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Rano_Raraku_quarry.jpg/250px-Rano_Raraku_quarry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rano_Raraku_quarry.jpg)

[Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island"), deforested.

Just as archaeologists have shown that prehistoric farming societies had to cut or burn forests before planting, documents and artifacts from early civilizations often reveal histories of deforestation. Some of the most dramatic are eighth century BCE Assyrian [reliefs](/wiki/Assyrian_Timber_Transportation_relief "Assyrian Timber Transportation relief") depicting logs being floated downstream from conquered areas to the less forested capital region as spoils of war. Ancient Chinese texts make clear that some areas of the Yellow River valley had already destroyed many of their forests over 2000 years ago and had to plant trees as crops or import them from long distances.[[309]](#cite_note-309) In South China much of the land came to be privately owned and used for the commercial growing of timber.[[310]](#cite_note-310)

Three regional studies of historic erosion and alluviation in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") found that, wherever adequate evidence exists, a major phase of erosion follows the introduction of farming in the various regions of Greece by about 500–1,000 years, ranging from the later Neolithic to the Early Bronze Age.[[311]](#cite_note-311) The thousand years following the mid-first millennium BC saw serious, intermittent pulses of soil erosion in numerous places. The historic [silting](/wiki/Silting "Silting") of ports along the southern coasts of [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor") (*e.g.* [Clarus](/wiki/Clarus "Clarus"), and the examples of [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus"), [Priene](/wiki/Priene "Priene") and [Miletus](/wiki/Miletus "Miletus"), where harbors had to be abandoned because of the silt deposited by the Meander) and in coastal [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") during the last centuries BC.[[312]](#cite_note-312)[[313]](#cite_note-313)

[Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island") has suffered from heavy [soil erosion](/wiki/Soil_erosion "Soil erosion") in recent centuries, aggravated by agriculture and deforestation.[[314]](#cite_note-314) The disappearance of the island's trees seems to coincide with a decline of its civilization around the 17th and 18th century. Scholars have attributed the collapse to deforestation and over-exploitation of all resources.[[315]](#cite_note-315)[[316]](#cite_note-316)

The famous silting up of the harbor for [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), which moved port commerce to [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), also followed a period of increased settlement growth (and apparently of deforestation) in the upper river basins. In early medieval [Riez](/wiki/Riez "Riez") in upper [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), alluvial silt from two small rivers raised the riverbeds and widened the floodplain, which slowly buried the Roman settlement in alluvium and gradually moved new construction to higher ground; concurrently the headwater valleys above Riez were being opened to pasturage.[[317]](#cite_note-317)

A typical [progress trap](/wiki/Progress_trap "Progress trap") was that cities were often built in a forested area, which would provide wood for some industry (for example, construction, shipbuilding, pottery). When deforestation occurs without proper replanting, however; local wood supplies become difficult to obtain near enough to remain competitive, leading to the city's abandonment, as happened repeatedly in Ancient [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor"). Because of fuel needs, mining and metallurgy often led to deforestation and city abandonment.[[318]](#cite_note-318)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Rugendas_-_Defrichement_d_une_Foret.jpg/250px-Rugendas_-_Defrichement_d_une_Foret.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugendas_-_Defrichement_d_une_Foret.jpg)

[Slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil") clearing the [Atlantic Forest](/wiki/Atlantic_Forest "Atlantic Forest") in Brazil, c. 1820–1825

With most of the population remaining active in (or indirectly dependent on) the agricultural sector, the main pressure in most areas remained land [clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(geography) "Clearing (geography)") for crop and cattle farming. Enough wild green was usually left standing (and partially used, for example, to collect firewood, timber and fruits, or to graze pigs) for wildlife to remain viable. The elite's (nobility and higher clergy) protection of their own hunting privileges and game often protected significant woodland.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Major parts in the spread (and thus more durable growth) of the population were played by monastical 'pioneering' (especially by the [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") and [Commercial](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce") orders) and some [feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") lords' recruiting farmers to settle (and become tax payers) by offering relatively good legal and fiscal conditions. Even when speculators sought to encourage towns, settlers needed an agricultural belt around or sometimes within defensive walls. When populations were quickly decreased by causes such as the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), the [colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas#Disease_and_indigenous_population_loss "European colonization of the Americas"),[[319]](#cite_note-Brierley_et_al_2019-319) or devastating warfare (for example, [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan")'s [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") hordes in eastern and central Europe, [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") in Germany), this could lead to settlements being abandoned. The land was reclaimed by nature, but the [secondary forests](/wiki/Secondary_forest "Secondary forest") usually lacked the original [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"). The [Mongol invasions and conquests](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_and_conquests "Mongol invasions and conquests") alone resulted in the reduction of 700 million tons of carbon from the atmosphere by enabling the re-growth of carbon-absorbing forests on depopulated lands over a significant period of time.[[320]](#cite_note-320)[[321]](#cite_note-321)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Tropenmuseum_Royal_Tropical_Institute_Objectnumber_60009006_Goudwinning_door_middel_van_het_afspo.jpg/250px-Tropenmuseum_Royal_Tropical_Institute_Objectnumber_60009006_Goudwinning_door_middel_van_het_afspo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tropenmuseum_Royal_Tropical_Institute_Objectnumber_60009006_Goudwinning_door_middel_van_het_afspo.jpg)

Deforestation in [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname") c. 1880–1900

From 1100 to 1500 AD, significant deforestation took place in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") as a result of the [expanding human population](/wiki/Human_overpopulation "Human overpopulation").[[322]](#cite_note-322) The large-scale building of wooden sailing ships by European (coastal) naval owners since the 15th century for exploration, [colonisation](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), [slave trade](/wiki/History_of_slavery "History of slavery"), and other trade on the high seas, consumed many forest resources and became responsible for the introduction of numerous [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") outbreaks in the 14th century. [Piracy](/wiki/Piracy "Piracy") also contributed to the over harvesting of forests, as in Spain. This led to a weakening of the domestic economy after Columbus' discovery of America, as the economy became dependent on colonial activities (plundering, mining, cattle, plantations, trade, etc.)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The massive use of [charcoal](/wiki/Charcoal "Charcoal") on an industrial scale in [Early Modern Europe](/wiki/Early_Modern_Europe "Early Modern Europe") was a new type of consumption of western forests.[[323]](#cite_note-323) Each of Nelson's [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") war ships at Trafalgar (1805) required 6,000 mature oaks for its construction.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In France, [Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert") planted [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") forests to supply the French navy in the future. When the oak plantations matured in the mid-19th century, the masts were no longer required because shipping had changed.[[324]](#cite_note-324)

Efforts to stop or slow deforestation have been attempted for many centuries because it has long been known that deforestation can cause environmental damage sufficient in some cases to cause societies to collapse. In [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"), paramount rulers developed policies designed to prevent conflicts between short-term gains from converting forest to farmland and long-term problems forest loss would cause,[[325]](#cite_note-325) while during the 17th and 18th centuries in [Tokugawa](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), Japan,[[326]](#cite_note-326) the shōguns developed a highly sophisticated system of long-term planning to stop and even reverse deforestation of the preceding centuries through substituting timber by other products and more efficient use of land that had been farmed for many centuries.

In 16th-century Germany, landowners also developed [silviculture](/wiki/Silviculture "Silviculture") to deal with the problem of deforestation. However, these policies tend to be limited to environments with *good rainfall*, *no dry season* and *very young [soils](/wiki/Soil "Soil")* (through [volcanism](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") or [glaciation](/wiki/Glaciation "Glaciation")). This is because on older and less fertile soils trees grow too slowly for silviculture to be economic, whilst in areas with a strong dry season there is always a risk of forest fires destroying a tree crop before it matures.

### 19th and 20th centuries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Burma024.jpg/250px-Burma024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burma024.jpg)

Deforestation in Burma (now [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")) *circa* 1920, during the [British colonial era](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma")

See also: [Deforestation by continent](/wiki/Deforestation_by_continent "Deforestation by continent")

#### Steamboats

In the 19th century, the introduction of [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was the cause of deforestation of banks of major rivers, such as the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), with increased and more severe flooding one of the environmental results. The steamboat crews cut wood daily from the riverbanks to fuel the steam engines. Between [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") and the confluence with the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") to the south, the Mississippi became broader and shallower and changed its channel laterally. Attempts to improve navigation by the use of [snag pullers](/wiki/Snagboat "Snagboat") often resulted in crews' [clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(geography) "Clearing (geography)") large trees 100 to 200 feet (61 m) back from the banks. Several French colonial towns of the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"), such as [Kaskaskia](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"), [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia,_Illinois "Cahokia, Illinois") and St. Philippe, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), were flooded and abandoned in the late 19th century, with a loss to the cultural record of their [archeology](/wiki/Archeology "Archeology").[[327]](#cite_note-327)

## Society and culture

Different cultures of different places in the world have different interpretations of the actions of the cutting down of trees. For example, in [Meitei mythology](/wiki/Meitei_mythology "Meitei mythology") and [Meitei folklore](/wiki/Meitei_folklore "Meitei folklore") of [Manipur](/wiki/Manipur "Manipur") (India), deforestation is mentioned as one of the reasons to make [mother nature](/wiki/Mother_nature "Mother nature") weep and mourn for the death of her precious children.[[328]](#cite_note-328)[[329]](#cite_note-329)[[330]](#cite_note-330)
